one_line_takeaway: A benchmark showing that eight off-the-shelf image and text watermarks are alarmingly fragile, with detection accuracy dropping 60-99% under ordinary real-world image corruptions and text edits.
key_finding: All eight watermarking schemes are highly fragile to routine perturbations: detection accuracy collapses from near-perfect on clean content (roughly 88-100%) to a fraction of that after perturbation, with detection-accuracy drops commonly in the -60% to -99% range. Text watermarks are hit hardest by character-level edits and least by sentence-level style/paraphrase transforms; Unigram-WM is by far the most brittle (detection drops of -82% up to -99.72%, e.g. Qwen-VL falling from 99.03% to 0.28%). For images, noise and blur (Zoom Blur worst, Glass Blur least harmful) are the most destructive. The paper concludes RivaGAN-WM is the comparatively most robust image watermark and KGW-WM the most robust text watermark; among generators, SDXL-Lightning (image) and LLaVA (text) yield the most robustness-friendly content.
core_contribution: The paper builds and runs a benchmark measuring how well multimodal (image and text) watermarks survive common real-world corruptions/perturbations. Its own contribution is the empirical evaluation harness plus the resulting findings, not any new watermarking method: it embeds 4 existing image watermarks (DwtDctSvd-WM, RivaGAN-WM, SSL-WM, StegaStamp-WM) and 4 existing text watermarks (KGW-WM, KTH-WM, Blackbox-WM, Unigram-WM) into content produced by 8 text-to-image models and 8 image-to-text models, then subjects that content to a curated bank of 100 image perturbations (20 corruption types x 5 severity levels, spanning noise/blur/weather/digital/geometric) and 63 text perturbations (19 techniques at character/word/sentence level) and measures how much watermark detection degrades.
threat_model: The paper's own threat framing (formalized in its Preliminary section, Defs 3.1-3.2) is a watermark-evasion setting: a detector Detect(x, aux) must distinguish watermarked from non-watermarked content while keeping Type I (false positive) and Type II (false negative) errors near zero; an 'attack' is any post-processing of possibly-watermarked content that shifts the watermarked/clean distributions so as to raise both error rates and evade detection. In practice the paper instantiates this threat with non-adversarial, real-world-style corruptions (compression, blur, noise, cropping/geometric transforms for images; typos, synonym/paraphrase, style transfer for text) rather than optimized adversarial attacks, treating benign distribution shift as the perturbation source. The security stakes are copyright assertion, authenticity verification, and AI-generated-content provenance/detection.
limitations: Explicitly a preliminary benchmark: it covers only image and text (no audio/video), only benign real-world corruptions (adversarial/optimization-based watermark-removal attacks are excluded because they target a classification-with-label setting the authors deem inapplicable), and the authors call model-level rankings 'speculative and not conclusive' since results can shift as the embedded watermark changes. There is an internal inconsistency the reviewer should flag: the introduction bullet claims SSL-WM is the most stable image watermark and KGW-WM the most stable text watermark, whereas the Results, Takeaway, and Conclusion instead name RivaGAN-WM as the most stable image watermark (KGW-WM is consistently the text winner). No standard external evaluation dataset is named; content is self-generated by the 16 models, and StegaStamp-WM's very low PSNR (~6-8 dB) suggests severe base image degradation that complicates its comparison.

datasets_used: Self-generated content (no external benchmark dataset named): images from 8 text-to-image models and text from 8 image-to-text models
Image perturbation bank: 20 corruption types x 5 severities = 100 image perturbations (noise/blur/weather/digital/geometric), adapted from Hendrycks & Dietterich and Qiu et al.
Text perturbation bank: 19 techniques (6 character-level, 5 word-level via EDA/AEDA, 8 sentence-level) totaling 63 perturbation methods with severity levels
quantitative_results: Unigram-WM is the most fragile text watermark: detection accuracy drops of -82.22% to -99.72% (Qwen-VL 99.03% -> 0.28%; InternLM-XComposer 100.00% -> 0.32%; Fuyu 100.00% -> 0.77%)
KGW-WM (most robust text WM) still drops substantially: e.g. LLaVA 1.5 98.04% -> 71.38% (-37.93%, its best case) but InternLM-XComposer 100.00% -> 14.97% (-85.03%)
DwtDctSvd-WM image detection drops -68.34% to -91.79% (Kandinsky 2.2 91.42% -> 7.51% = -91.79%; Stable Diffusion 100.00% -> 15.79% = -84.21%)
RivaGAN-WM (most robust image WM) drops least among image methods, e.g. SDXL-Lightning 100.00% -> 36.83% (-63.17%), LCMs 99.00% -> 35.84% (-63.80%)
StegaStamp-WM shows both poor base image quality (PSNR ~5.8-8.2 dB, SSIM ~0.21-0.29) and large detection drops -76.59% to -89.11% (RPG-Image 80.26% -> 8.74% = -89.11%)
Clean/original detection accuracy is near-perfect across the board (image ~80-100%, text ~97.4-100%) before perturbation
baselines_compared: Image watermarks: DwtDctSvd-WM, RivaGAN-WM, SSL-WM, StegaStamp-WM
Text watermarks: KGW-WM, KTH-WM, Blackbox-WM, Unigram-WM
Text-to-image generators: NExT-GPT, Stable Diffusion, DALLE3, SDXL-Lightning, PIXART, Kandinsky 2.2, LCMs, RPG
Image-to-text generators: NExT-GPT, Fuyu-8B, InternLM-XComposer, InstructBLIP, LLaVA 1.6, MiniGPT-4, mPLUG-Owl2, Qwen-VL
